3: qol qore bamidbar panu derek YHWH yasheru baaravah mesillah lelohenu (qôl qôrēʾ bammidbar pannû derek Yhwh yašĕrû bāʿărābāh mĕsillāh lēʾlōhênû): 'A voice crying in the wilderness: Prepare the way of the LORD; make straight in the desert a highway for our God.' The four Gospels unanimously apply this voice to John the Baptist. Critically, the original syntax is ambiguous: is the voice 'in the wilderness' (most translations) or does the voice cry 'Prepare the way of the LORD in the wilderness' (Mark 1:3 seems to read the wilderness phrase with the road-preparation)? The Qumran community (1QS Isaiah 8:14) cited this same verse for their own desert withdrawal — interpreting 'preparing the way' as Torah-study. The NT's application to John is the contested alternative: the way is prepared through proclamation, not desert community-formation.
1: naḥămû naḥămû ʿammî — "Comfort, comfort my people" — the opening imperative of the great prologue, doubled for emphasis (infinitive absolute construction: absolute certainty). The structural break from Isaiah 1-39 is marked by content, not form — no superscription, but a complete change of tone from judgment to consolation. yōʾmar ʾĕlōhêkem — "says your God" — the comfort is commanded by God and addressed to God's own people in the possessive (ʿammî, "my people").
2: dibbĕrû ʿal-lēb yĕrûšālaim — "speak to the heart of Jerusalem" — the intimate address formula (dibbēr ʿal-lēb, to speak to the heart, used in courtship: Genesis 34:3; Ruth 2:13; Hosea 2:14). The comfort has the character of a lover's tender speech, not a legal announcement. ṣĕbāʾāh mālĕʾâ — "her hard service is completed" (ṣāḇāʾ, H6635, army/forced service); nirsâ ʿăwōnāh — "her iniquity is pardoned/accepted." kî lāqĕḥâ miyyad Yhwh kiflayim — "she has received from YHWH's hand double for all her sins" — the double payment formula implies both the debt is fully settled and the grace exceeds the requirement.
4: kol-gay yinnāśēʾ wĕkol-har wĕgibʿâ yišpālû — "every valley will be lifted up, and every mountain and hill made low" — the cosmogonic leveling of creation to prepare the divine highway (derekh). The reversal of terrain — valley elevated, mountain lowered — describes not physical geography but the removal of all obstacles to YHWH's coming. wĕhāyâ heʿāqōb lĕmîšôr — "the rough ground will become level" (mîšôr, H4334, a level plain).
5: wĕniglâ kĕbôd Yhwh — "the glory of YHWH will be revealed" (gālâ, niphal — disclosed, unveiled). wĕrāʾû kol-bāśār yaḥdāw — "and all flesh will see it together" — the revelation of divine glory to all humanity simultaneously and without exception (kol-bāśār, all flesh). kî pî Yhwh dibber — "for the mouth of YHWH has spoken" — the guarantee is the divine speech-act itself, which cannot be undone.
6: qôl ʾōmēr qĕrāʾ wĕʾāmar mā ʾeqrāʾ — "a voice says 'Call out!' and one asks 'What shall I call out?'" — the dialogue introducing the grass/flower oracle. kol-habbāśār ḥāṣîr wĕkol-ḥasdô kĕṣîṣ haśśādeh — "all flesh is grass, and all its steadfast love/glory is like the flower of the field." The word ḥeseḏ here is unusual in this context — it is usually YHWH's characteristic; applied to human beings it may be ironic: what humans regard as their greatest virtue is as ephemeral as a wildflower. 1 Peter 1:24-25 quotes Isaiah 40:6-8.
7: yābēš ḥāṣîr nābēl ṣîṣ kî rûaḥ Yhwh nāšĕpâ bô — "the grass withers, the flower fades when the breath/Spirit of YHWH blows on it" — YHWH's rûaḥ both gives life (Genesis 2:7) and withdraws it. The rûaḥ here is the scorching desert wind, but also implicitly the divine breath that makes life contingent. ʾākēn ḥāṣîr hāʿām — "surely the people are grass" — the application to humanity is explicit. All human civilization is as impermanent as vegetation.
8: yābēš ḥāṣîr nābēl ṣîṣ — "the grass withers, the flower fades" — repeated from Isaiah 40:7 for emphasis and then completed with the contrast: ûdĕbar ʾĕlōhênû yāqûm lĕʿôlām — "but the word of our God stands forever." The contrast is absolute: created matter is ephemeral; divine word is permanent. 1 Peter 1:25 applies this to the gospel: "this word is the good news that was preached to you." The eternal word takes the form of the preached gospel.
9: mĕbaśśeret ṣiyyôn — "herald of good news to Zion" — the root bāśar (H1319) is the verb underlying bĕśôrâ (good news) and the Greek evangelion. The herald who brings good news of YHWH's coming is the precursor to the NT concept of gospel proclamation. rômî bakkōaḥ qôleḵ — "lift up your voice with strength" — the herald is commanded to amplify, not whisper. ʾimrî lĕʿārê yĕhûdâ hinnēh ʾĕlōhêkem — "say to the cities of Judah, 'Behold, your God!'" — the content of the gospel is the announcement of God's arrival.
10: hinnēh ʾădōnāy Yhwh bĕḥāzāq yābōʾ — "behold, Lord YHWH comes with might/power" — the coming is not quiet but powerful (bĕḥāzāq, with strength). ûzrōʿô mōšlâ lô — "his arm rules for him" — the strong arm of YHWH is the instrument of sovereign rule. hinnēh śĕkārô ʾittô ûpĕullātô lĕpānāyw — "his reward (śākār) is with him, and his recompense (pĕullâ) before him" — the formula repeated in 62:11 and Revelation 22:12 where Christ comes with his recompense.
11: kĕrōʿeh ʿēḏrô yirʿeh bizrōʿô yĕqabbēṣ ṭĕlāʾîm — "like a shepherd he pastures his flock; with his arm he gathers the lambs." The contrast with Isaiah 40:10's powerful arm: the same arm that rules (Isaiah 40:10) is the arm that gathers (qābaṣ) the lambs. ûbĕḥêqô yiśśāʾ — "and carries them in his bosom/breast" — the image of divine tenderness in the context of sovereign power. The shepherd imagery here is taken up in John 10:11 and Luke 15:4-7. ʿālôt yĕnahēl — "he gently leads the nursing ewes."
12: mî-māddad bĕšāʿlô mayim — "who has measured the waters in the hollow of his hand?" (šaʿal, the cupped palm — measuring water in the hollow of one's hand). wĕkāl baśśālîš ʿāpār hāʾāreṣ — "and marked off the heavens with a span" (šālîš, H8027, a third — the unit of the hand-span), wĕkāl baśśālîš ʿāpār hāʾāreṣ — "and enclosed the dust of the earth in a measure." The creation-scale measurements are expressed in human body-measure terms — the cosmic is rendered comprehensible through embodied metaphor.
13: mî-tikkēn ʾet-rûaḥ Yhwh — "who has measured/directed the Spirit of YHWH?" (tākan, H8505, to regulate, measure, direct). wĕʾîš ʿăṣātô yôdîʿennû — "and what counselor (ʾîš ʿăṣâ) made him know?" Paul quotes this in Romans 11:34 ("Who has known the mind of the Lord, or who has been his counselor?") and 1 Corinthians 2:16 ("who has known the mind of the Lord so as to instruct him?"). The independence of divine wisdom from human instruction is absolute — YHWH was not taught by anyone.
14: ʾet-mî nôʿāṣ wayyĕbînēhû — "with whom did he take counsel, and who made him understand?" — the series of rhetorical questions continues. wayyĕlammdēhû bĕʾōraḥ mišpāṭ — "who taught him the path of justice?" None. The point is YHWH's absolute independence of mind — he was not mentored in wisdom, justice, or knowledge by any created being. His wisdom is not derived but original.
15: hēn gôyim kĕmar middĕlî — "behold, the nations are like a drop from a bucket" (mar, a drip/drop; dĕlî, a bucket/vessel for drawing water). ûkĕšaḥaq mōzĕnayim neḥšĕḇû — "reckoned as dust on the scales" (šaḥaq, fine dust, the kind too light to register on a balance). The political powers that overwhelm human experience are, from YHWH's perspective, negligible. The cosmic creator views the greatest empires as irrelevant weight.
16: ûlĕḇānôn ʾên dî bāʿēr — "Lebanon is not sufficient for fuel" — even the endless cedars of Lebanon would not produce enough wood for a sacrifice worthy of YHWH. wĕḥayyātô ʾên dî ʿôlâ — "and its animals are not sufficient for a burnt offering." The point is not that sacrifice is useless but that no created abundance is adequate to honor the Creator — the gap between creature and Creator is infinite.
17: kol-haggôyim kĕʾayim negdô — "all the nations are as nothing before him" — ʾayim (H657, H408) — nothingness, non-existence. mēʾepes wātōhû neḥšĕḇû-lô — "they are accounted as less than nothing (ʾepes, zero/end) and emptiness (tōhû, the same as Genesis 1:2's primeval chaos) to him." The nations are not merely small — they are below the threshold of being. YHWH's perspective reduces all geopolitical significance to zero.
18: wĕʾel-mî tĕdammĕyûn ʾēl — "to whom then will you liken God (ʾēl)?" — the decisive anti-idol question. ûmah-dĕmût taʿărĕkû lô — "what likeness will you set up to compare with him?" The question opens the idol polemic that Isaiah 40-48 develops at length (cf. Isaiah 44:9-20; Isaiah 46:1-13). Any human representation of YHWH is by definition inadequate — not merely aesthetically limited but ontologically impossible.
19: The idol's manufacture: happesel nāsak ḥārāš — "the craftsman casts an idol" (pesel, carved/cast image; nāsak, to pour/cast molten metal). wĕṣōrēp bazzāhāb yĕraqqĕʿennû — "the goldsmith overlays it with gold" (rāqaʿ, to hammer thin, to plate). ûrĕtuqôt kesep ṣōrēp — "the silversmith casts chains of silver" — the finishing work of the idol production. The detailed manufacturing description makes the point: this thing was made by human hands, step by step. It is entirely a human product.
20: hamesukkān tĕrûmâ — "the one who is too poor for a dedicatory offering" (sukkān, H5539, impoverished — debated) selects wood that will not rot (ʿēṣ lōʾ-yirqāb). He seeks a skilled craftsman (ḥākām) to set up an idol that will not move (lōʾ-yimmôṭ). The irony is the goal: the idol's primary virtue is that it stays put — it does not move. YHWH's defining characteristic is that he acts, speaks, and intervenes. The static stability of the idol is the exact opposite of the dynamic activity of YHWH.
21: hălōʾ tēdĕʿû hălōʾ tišmāʿû — "do you not know? Do you not hear?" — four rhetorical questions challenging the idol-makers' ignorance. hălōʾ huggad lākem mērōʾš — "was it not told you from the beginning?" hălōʾ hĕḇînottem môsĕdôt hāʾāreṣ — "have you not understood from the foundations of the earth?" The knowledge of YHWH's creative sovereignty is not new revelation but primordial truth available to all — the idol-maker's error is culpable ignorance, not innocent confusion.
22: hayyōšēḇ ʿal-ḥûg hāʾāreṣ — "he who sits above the circle of the earth" (ḥûg, H2329, circle/vault/arc). wĕyōšĕḇêhā kĕḥăgāḇîm — "and its inhabitants are like grasshoppers" — the divine perspective on humanity. From YHWH's cosmic vantage point, the great nations and their inhabitants are as small and numerous as grasshoppers in a field. hannōṭeh kaḏōq šāmayim — "who stretches out the heavens like a curtain" (dōq, H1852, thin cloth/gauze).
23: hannōtēn rôzĕnîm lāʾayin — "who brings princes to nothing" — rôzĕnîm (H7336, rulers/princes, from rāzan, to be weighty/authoritative). šōpĕṭê ʾāreṣ kattōhû ʿāśâ — "makes the judges of the earth as nothing (tōhû)." The application of the creation vocabulary of Genesis 1:2 (tōhû) to human rulers is pointed: the great political powers are returned to primeval formlessness by YHWH's action.
24: ʾap bal-niṭṭāʿû ʾap bal-zōrāʿû ʾap bal-šōrēš bāʾāreṣ gizʿām — "scarcely planted, scarcely sown, scarcely has their stem taken root in the earth" — the three ʾap bal ("hardly... not yet") constructions describe the briefness of a ruler's establishment. wayyiggaʿ bāhem wayyi bāšû — "he blows on them and they wither" — YHWH's breath (nāšap, blow) is all it takes to end a reign. ûsĕʿārâ kaqaš tiśśāʾēm — "and a whirlwind carries them off like stubble."
25: wĕʾel-mî tĕdammĕyûnî wĕʾeśweh — "to whom then will you compare me, that I should be like him?" — YHWH himself asks the anti-idol question (the same as Isaiah 40:18 but now in the first person). yōʾmar qādôš — "says the Holy One." The divine self-designation qādôš (Holy One — Isaiah's signature divine title: Isaiah 1:4; Isaiah 5:19,24; Isaiah 6:3; Isaiah 10:17; Isaiah 12:6; Isaiah 17:7; etc.) reasserts incomparability from YHWH's own mouth.
26: śĕʾû mārôm ʿênêkem ûrĕʾû mî-bārāʾ ʾēlleh — "lift up your eyes on high and see: who created these?" — the command to look at the stars as evidence for the Creator. hammôṣiʾ bĕmispār ṣĕbāʾām — "who brings out their host by number" (ṣĕbāʾ, army/host — the stars as YHWH's cosmic army mustered and reviewed). lĕkullām bĕšēm yiqrāʾ — "to all of them he calls by name" — the naming of each star by YHWH is both the assertion of sovereignty and of intimate knowledge. mērōb ʾônîm wĕʾammîṣ kōaḥ ʾîš lōʾ neʿĕdār — "because of his great might, and the strength of his power, not one is missing."
27: lāmmâ tōʾmar yaʿăqōb ûtĕdabbēr yiśrāʾēl nistarrâ darkî mēyhwh — "why do you say, O Jacob, and speak, O Israel, 'My way is hidden from YHWH, and my right/justice is passed over by my God'?" — the despairing complaint of exile: YHWH does not see our suffering; our case goes unheard. The questions are not accusations but pastoral address — YHWH engages the people's doubt, not dismissing it but answering it.
28: hălōʾ yāḏaʿtā ʾim-lōʾ šāmaʿtā — "Have you not known? Have you not heard?" — the same rhetorical questions as Isaiah 40:21, now applied to the Israelites themselves. ʾĕlōhê ʿôlām Yhwh bōrēʾ qĕṣôt hāʾāreṣ — "YHWH is the everlasting God (ʾĕlōhê ʿôlām), the Creator of the ends of the earth." lōʾ yîʿap wĕlōʾ yîgāʿ — "he does not faint or grow weary" — the same verbs used of the exhausted exiles in Isaiah 40:29-31 are denied of YHWH. His inexhaustible energy is the source of renewed energy for the weary.
29: nōtēn lĕyāʿēp kōaḥ — "he gives power to the faint/exhausted" (yāʿēp, H3287, exhausted). ûlĕʾên ʾônîm ʿaṣmâ yarbeh — "and to him who has no might he increases strength" (ʾôn, H202, strength, vigor — "no strength" is the absolute exhaustion of exile). The divine pattern: YHWH gives to those who have nothing, increases to those who lack. This is not a reward for the strong but a gift to the weak — the theology of grace in concentrated form.
30: yîʿăpû nĕʿārîm wĕyîgāʿû — "even youths (nāʿar) will faint and grow weary" — the young, who should have the most energy, are not exempt from exhaustion. ûbaḥûrîm kāšôl yikkāšēlû — "and young men (bāḥûr, choice/selected young men, the military elite) will fall exhausted." Human strength at its peak cannot sustain itself indefinitely. The point is not pessimism but preparation for the contrast in Isaiah 40:31.
31: wĕqôwê Yhwh yaḥălîpû kōaḥ — "but those who wait on/hope in YHWH will renew/exchange strength" (qāwâ, H6960, to wait, to hope with expectant tension — cf. Isaiah 25:9; Isaiah 26:8; Isaiah 33:2; Isaiah 49:23). The verb ḥālaP (H2498, to exchange, to renew, to sprout fresh) suggests not merely restoration but transformation — new strength replacing old. yaʿălû ʾēḇer kannĕšārîm — "they will soar on wings like eagles" (nešer, the large bird of prey — eagle or vulture). yārûṣû wĕlōʾ yîgāʿû yēlĕkû wĕlōʾ yîʿāpû — "they will run and not grow weary, they will walk and not faint" — the progression moves from soaring (extraordinary) to running (normal effort) to walking (ordinary pace), all sustained by YHWH's energy.
